name: Hashing (Maps & Sets)
concept:
## The idea

A **hash map** (Python `dict`) and **hash set** (Python `set`) give you average **O(1)** lookup, insert, and membership tests. The core move in this pattern: *trade memory for time* — remember what you've seen so you don't have to re-scan.

The classic transformation: a brute-force O(n²) 'for each element, look through all the others' becomes O(n) 'for each element, check a dict in O(1)'.

## Python you'll pick up here

- `seen = set()`, `seen.add(x)`, `if x in seen:`
- `d = {}` or `d = {key: value}`; `d[key] = value`; `if key in d:`
- `from collections import Counter` → `Counter(s)` counts characters/elements in one line
- `from collections import defaultdict` → `d = defaultdict(int)` auto-initializes missing keys to 0
- `d.get(key, default)` reads with a fallback

These four idioms (`set`, `dict`, `Counter`, `defaultdict`) cover a huge fraction of interview code.
whenToUse: You catch yourself writing a nested loop just to check 'have I seen this?'
You need counts / frequencies of elements or characters
You need to detect duplicates, or find a complement (target - x)
You need to group items by some key

problems.title: Contains Duplicate
problems.keyIdeas: Walk the array, keeping a `set` of everything seen. If you ever meet a number already in the set, there's a duplicate. This is the 'remember what you've seen' idea in its purest form.
hints: Sorting would work in O(n log n). Can you do better with extra memory?
Keep a set of numbers you've already encountered.
Before adding each number, check if it's already in the set — if so, return True.

solutions.label: Hash set
solutions.keyIdea: First repeat you encounter means a duplicate exists.

solutions.label: One-liner (learn the idiom)
solutions.keyIdea: A set drops duplicates, so if the set is smaller than the list, there was a repeat.

solutions.label: Alternative — sort
solutions.keyIdea: Sort and check adjacent pairs. Reach for this when you can't afford O(n) extra space — sort is O(n log n) time but only O(1) space if you mutate in-place (Python's sort does). Use hashing when speed matters more than space.

problems.description: Given an integer array nums, return true if any value appears at least twice in the array, and return false if every element is distinct.

problems.title: Valid Anagram
problems.keyIdeas: Two strings are anagrams iff they have the **same character counts**. Count letters in each with a hash map and compare. `collections.Counter` does this in one line — a great Python idiom to internalize.
hints: Anagrams have identical letter frequencies. How would you represent 'letter frequencies'?
Count each character of both strings into two dicts, then compare the dicts.
`collections.Counter(s) == Counter(t)` is the whole solution once you see it.

solutions.label: Counter (idiomatic Python)
solutions.keyIdea: Equal character-frequency maps ⇔ anagram.

solutions.label: Manual dict (see what Counter does)
solutions.keyIdea: Build the count map by hand: add for s, subtract for t; all-zero means anagram.

solutions.label: Alternative — sort
solutions.keyIdea: Anagrams have identical sorted forms. This avoids extra space (O(1) if sorting in-place) but costs O(n log n) time. Reach for sorting when memory is tight; use Counter when speed matters.

problems.description: Given two strings s and t, return true if t is an anagram of s, and false otherwise.

problems.title: Two Sum
problems.keyIdeas: For each number `x`, the partner you need is `target - x`. Keep a dict of `value -> index` as you go; if the needed complement is already in the dict, you're done. One pass, O(n) — the canonical example of hashing beating a nested loop.
hints: Brute force tries every pair in O(n²). What if, for each number, you could instantly ask 'have I already seen its complement?'
As you scan, store each value's index in a dict.
For value x, the complement is target - x. If that complement is already a key in your dict, return the two indices.

solutions.label: Brute force
solutions.keyIdea: Check every pair (i, j).

solutions.label: Optimal — hash map, one pass
solutions.keyIdea: Remember each value's index; look up the complement in O(1).

problems.description: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target.

problems.title: Group Anagrams
problems.keyIdeas: Anagrams share the same sorted signature (or character-count tuple). Build a hash map `signature -> [words]`, then return the values. The insight: two strings are anagrams iff `sorted(s) == sorted(t)`.
hints: Two anagrams will have identical sorted forms ('eat' → 'aet', 'tea' → 'aet').
Group by sorted string as a key in a dict.
Use `collections.defaultdict(list)` so you can append directly without checking if the key exists.

solutions.label: Optimal — sort as key
solutions.keyIdea: Sorted string is the canonical signature; group by it in O(n k log k) where k is max word length.

solutions.label: Optimal — count tuple as key
solutions.keyIdea: Count-tuple avoids the sort; slightly faster constant but same complexity.

problems.description: Given an array of strings strs, group the anagrams together. You can return the answer in any order.

problems.title: Top K Frequent Elements
problems.keyIdeas: Count frequencies with a hash map. Then use **bucket sort**: create buckets `freq[i] = [elements with count i]` and collect the top k from the highest-frequency buckets downward. This beats a heap (O(n log n)) with O(n).
hints: First, count each element's frequency with Counter.
A heap would give O(n log k) — but can you do O(n)?
Bucket sort: make an array of length n+1 where index i holds all elements with frequency i. Walk from the end.

solutions.label: Better — sort by frequency
solutions.keyIdea: Count frequencies, then sort by count descending. Simple and correct in O(n log n). Use this when n is small or when you want readable code; reach for heap when k << n, or bucket sort for guaranteed O(n).
solutions.code:
from collections import Counter

class Solution:
    def topKFrequent(self, nums: list[int], k: int) -> list[int]:
        count = Counter(nums)
        # sort by frequency descending
        sorted_items = sorted(count.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
        return [num for num, freq in sorted_items[:k]]

solutions.label: Better — heap
solutions.keyIdea: Count frequencies, then use a heap to extract k largest. O(n log k) with a size-k min-heap. Reach for this when k is small relative to n (e.g., k=10, n=1M) — beats sorting; use bucket sort when you need strict O(n).

solutions.label: Optimal — bucket sort
solutions.keyIdea: Bucket by frequency (max n), collect from high to low until you have k elements. O(n) time. Reach for this when you need the best asymptotic guarantee — no log factor — but the simpler heap or sort is often fine in practice.

problems.description: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the k most frequent elements. You may return the answer in any order.

problems.title: Product of Array Except Self

video.title: NeetCode — Product of Array Except Self (Python)

problems.keyIdeas: For each index i, the answer is (product of everything to its left) × (product of everything to its right). Precompute prefix and suffix products; multiply them. The O(1)-space trick: build the answer array as the prefix pass, then multiply in-place during the suffix pass.
hints: You can't use division (and there might be zeros). Think about what information you'd need at each index.
At index i, you need: (product left of i) × (product right of i).
Two passes: one left-to-right accumulating prefix, one right-to-left accumulating suffix. Can you merge them into the output array to save space?

solutions.label: Prefix + suffix arrays
solutions.keyIdea: Store prefix product in one array, suffix in another, then multiply at each index.

solutions.label: Optimal — O(1) space
solutions.keyIdea: Use the output array itself to hold prefix, then multiply suffix in-place on the second pass.
solutions.code:
class Solution:
    def productExceptSelf(self, nums: list[int]) -> list[int]:
        n = len(nums)
        result = [1] * n
        
        # first pass: result[i] = prefix product (everything left of i)
        prefix = 1
        for i in range(n):
            result[i] = prefix
            prefix *= nums[i]
        
        # second pass: multiply by suffix product (everything right of i)
        suffix = 1
        for i in range(n - 1, -1, -1):
            result[i] *= suffix
            suffix *= nums[i]
        
        return result

problems.description: Given an integer array nums, return an array answer such that answer[i] is equal to the product of all the elements of nums except nums[i].

problems.title: Valid Sudoku
problems.keyIdeas: Track three sets per digit: which rows, columns, and 3×3 boxes have seen it. The trick: map (row, col) to box index with `(r // 3, c // 3)`. One pass, check membership in O(1) each time — classic hashing.
hints: You need to check: no duplicate in any row, any column, or any 3×3 sub-box.
Keep a set for each row, each column, and each box. As you scan, check 'is this digit already in the row/col/box set?'
Box index: (r // 3, c // 3) maps cell (r, c) to one of 9 boxes.

solutions.label: Optimal — three sets
solutions.keyIdea: Use defaultdict(set) for rows, cols, boxes. Single pass, O(1) membership checks.

problems.description: Determine if a 9 x 9 Sudoku board is valid. Only the filled cells need to be validated according to the following rules:

problems.title: Encode and Decode Strings
problems.keyIdeas: You need a delimiter that won't collide with string content. The robust scheme: prefix each string with its length and a delimiter: `"4#word5#hello"`. Decode by reading length, skipping the `#`, then slicing exactly that many characters.
hints: A simple delimiter like ',' fails if the strings themselves contain commas.
Store the length of each string in the encoding, so you know how many characters to read.
Format: `len(s) + '#' + s` for each string. Decode: parse the length, skip '#', slice that many characters.

solutions.label: Optimal — length-prefix encoding
solutions.keyIdea: Encode as `len#str` for each string; decode by parsing length, then slicing.

problems.title: Longest Consecutive Sequence
problems.keyIdeas: Put everything in a set for O(1) lookups. For each number that could **start** a sequence (i.e., `n - 1` is not in the set), count how long the run `n, n+1, n+2, ...` goes. Only starting new sequences keeps it O(n) — each number is visited at most twice.
hints: Sorting would give O(n log n). Can you do O(n)?
Use a set for O(1) membership. For each number, check if it's the start of a sequence.
A number is the start of a sequence iff `num - 1` is NOT in the set. From there, count upward `num, num+1, ...` until you hit a gap.

solutions.label: Brute force — sort
solutions.keyIdea: Sort and scan for consecutive runs. O(n log n).

solutions.label: Optimal — hash set, start-of-sequence
solutions.keyIdea: Only start counting from numbers that begin a sequence (n-1 not in set). Each number visited ≤ twice → O(n). Reach for this when you need linear time and have O(n) space; use sort when you can't afford extra memory or when the data is nearly sorted.

solutions.label: Alternative — union-find
solutions.keyIdea: Build a union-find structure connecting consecutive numbers. For each number, union it with num-1 and num+1 if they exist. The largest component is the answer. Reach for this when you're already using union-find elsewhere (e.g., connected components) or when you want to practice the pattern; the hash-set approach is simpler for this problem.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(n α(n)) — α is inverse Ackermann, effectively O(n)

problems.description: Given an unsorted array of integers nums, return the length of the longest consecutive elements sequence.

problems.title: shortest Palindrome
hints: Consider the constraints
Think about the optimal approach

solutions.label: Solution from repo
solutions.keyIdea: TODO: Add key ideas
solutions.code:
s = "aabaabcd"
def shortestPalindrome(s):
    rollingHashesSet = set()
    rollingHash = 0
    n = len(s)
    d, q = 26, 101
    for i in range(0, n):
        rollingHash = (rollingHash*d+ord(s[i]))%q
        rollingHashesSet.add(rollingHash)
    print(rollingHashesSet)

    powerModMap = [1 for _ in range(n)]
    for i in range(1,n):
        powerModMap[i] = (powerModMap[i-1]*d)%q
    print(powerModMap)

    reverseRollingHash = 0
    maxLenPalindrome = 0
    revString = s[::-1]
    for i in range(n-1,-1,-1):
        reverseRollingHash = (reverseRollingHash + powerModMap[n-1-i]*ord(revString[i]))%q
        if reverseRollingHash in rollingHashesSet:
            if s[:n-i]==revString[i:]:
                maxLenPalindrome = max(maxLenPalindrome, n-i)
    return (revString[:n-maxLenPalindrome]+s)
    
print(shortestPalindrome(s))

def shortestPalindromeBest(s):
    n = len(s)
    d, q = 26, 101
    powMod = 1
    hashS = 0
    hashReverseS = 0
    reverseS = s[::-1]
    maxIndex = 0
    for i in range(n):
        hashS = (d*hashS + ord(s[i]))%q
        hashReverseS = (hashReverseS + ord(s[i])*powMod)%q
        if hashS==hashReverseS:
            if s[:i+1] == reverseS[n-i-1:]:
                maxIndex = max(maxIndex, i)
        powMod = (powMod*d)%q
    return reverseS[:n-maxIndex-1]+s
print(shortestPalindromeBest(s))

problems.description: You are given a string s. You can convert s to a palindrome by adding characters in front of it.
